coredata.dc:title: Yellow fluorescent carbon quantum dots for Ag+/GSH detection and differentiation between normal and cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Xiaokun
Lu, Xingjian
Zou, Hao
Hu, Xiaoyan
Xiong, Yue
Cao, Jun
Pan, Jiaqi
Li, Chaorong
Zheng, Yingying
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel multifunctional fluorescent carbon quantum dot (CDs) was prepared by the solvothermal method in which o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was used as the carbon source, p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) was used as the nitrogen source and ethanol was used as the reaction solvent. The prepared CDs possessed fluorescence “off” response toward silver ions (Ag+) with a minimum detection limit of 75 nM. Meanwhile, glutathione (GSH) as an amino acid could selectively restore the fluorescence performance of CDs quenched by Ag+ ions and showed good detection sensitivity (detection limit of 10 nM). Owing to the low cell toxicity and high biocompatibility, the application of CDs in cell imaging demonstrated that they could be used as a distinguishing recognition between normal cells (EC) and cancer cells (MCF-7) because of their highly precise sensing ability for GSH. All of the above indicated that the prepared CDs would have great application prospects in biological diagnosis and sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Cell imaging
Ag+ ion detection
GSH sensing
Normal/cancer cell distinction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective detection of Ag+ ions in different metal ion solutions
Selectivity detection of Ag+ ions by CDs
Detection of Ag+ and GSH in living cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51802282 ) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ17F040004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and HRTEM (inset) image, (b) size distribution of CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of CDs; (b) Full XPS spectrum of CDs; (c) High-resolution C1s spectrum of CDs; (d) High-resolution O1s spectrum of CDs; (e) High-resolution N1s spectrum of CDs.
(a) UV-Vis and PL excitation and emission spectra; (b)PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths of CDs; (c)Fluorescence intensity of CDs solutions at different pH (2.0–12.0) values.

Selectivity detection of Ag+ ions by CDs. (a) Influence of different metal ions on the fluorescence of CDs; (b) The quenching effect of Ag+ on the fluorescence of CDs in the presence of other metal ions (The error bars in the figure indicate the 3 sets of parallel sample errors for all experimental data).

Detection of Ag+ by CDs. (a) The effect of concentration of Ag+ (5–600 μM) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs (50 μg/mL). (b) Linear correlation diagram of F and concentration of Ag+ (5–500 μM) (F=f0/f, where f0 referred to the fluorescence intensity of CDs (50 μg/mL) at 560 nm, and f represented the fluorescence intensity after interacting with different concentrations of Ag+, C was the concentration of Ag+, R was the linear correlation coefficient). (c) Lifetime decay profiles of CDs and CDs/Ag+ system.

(a) The effect of different concentrations of GSH (0–165 μM) on fluorescence intensity in CDs/Ag+ system (50 μg/mL and 600 μM, respectively). (b) The linear correlation diagram of F and concentration of GSH) (F=f0/f, f0 represented the fluorescence intensity at 560 nm after interaction with different concentrations of GSH, f referred to the fluorescence intensity of 50 μg/mL CDs and 600 μM Ag+ system at 560 nm, C was the concentration of GSH, R was the linear correlation coefficient).
Viability of MCF-7and EC cell lines with different concentrations of CDs (0–150 μg/mL). (The error bars in the figure indicate the 3 sets of parallel sample errors for all experimental data).

Fluorescence imaging of MCF-7 cells (a) incubated with CDs (200 μg/mL); (b) incubated with CDs (200 μg/mL) and then Ag+(500 μM); and (c) incubated with CDs (200 μg/mL), then Ag+ (500 μM), and then GSH (200 μM).
Laser confocal images of MCF-7 and EC cells after treatment with CDs (200 μg/mL), Ag+ (200 μM) and GSH (200 μM)): (a) EC, (b) MCF-7.
Schematic illustration of CDs synthesis and fluorescence “on-off-on” sensing platform for detection of Ag+ ions and GSH.

head.ce:title: Yellow fluorescent carbon quantum dots for Ag+/GSH detection and differentiation between normal and cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optical Field Manipulation of Zhejiang Province, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Department of Physics, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optical Field Manipulation of Zhejiang Province, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Department of Physics, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optical Field Manipulation of Zhejiang Province, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Department of Physics, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel multifunctional fluorescent carbon quantum dot (CDs) was prepared by the solvothermal method in which o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was used as the carbon source, p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) was used as the nitrogen source and ethanol was used as the reaction solvent. The prepared CDs possessed fluorescence “off” response toward silver ions (Ag+) with a minimum detection limit of 75 nM. Meanwhile, glutathione (GSH) as an amino acid could selectively restore the fluorescence performance of CDs quenched by Ag+ ions and showed good detection sensitivity (detection limit of 10 nM). Owing to the low cell toxicity and high biocompatibility, the application of CDs in cell imaging demonstrated that they could be used as a distinguishing recognition between normal cells (EC) and cancer cells (MCF-7) because of their highly precise sensing ability for GSH. All of the above indicated that the prepared CDs would have great application prospects in biological diagnosis and sensing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel multifunctional fluorescent carbon quantum dot (CDs) was prepared by the solvothermal method in which o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was used as the carbon source, p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) was used as the nitrogen source and ethanol was used as the reaction solvent. The prepared CDs possessed fluorescence “off” response toward silver ions (Ag+), glutathione (GSH) as an amino acid could selectively restore the fluorescence performance of CDs quenched by Ag+ ions，CDs could be used as a distinguishing recognition between normal cells (EC) and cancer cells (MCF-7).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The application of conventional nanomaterial is restricted due to their biological hazards and inherent toxicity. Carbon dot (CDs) is a new type of zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterial with a diameter smaller than 10 nm [1,2]. Due to its good biocompatibility, minimal toxicity, optical properties, and perfect water dispersion [3–8], CDs has been widely used in biosensing, biological imaging, ion detection, photocatalysis, and drug delivery [9–12].

However, compared with conventional probes, CDs has the defects of low quantum yield, low fluorescence intensity, and fluorescence emission wavelengths of CDs are mostly focused in the blue-green range [13–15]. In order to solve the above problems, scientists have doped carbon dots using nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus atoms [16–18]. The quantum yield and luminescence properties of carbon dots were improved. Li et al. synthesized red-colored CDs (R-CDs) and their quantum yield (QY) was greatly improved to 12.1 %, and these R-CDs could be used to detect Au3+, Pt2+, and Pd2+ in aqueous media [19]. Li et al. prepared yellow CDs (F-CDs) with a fluorescence lifetime of 6.82 ns by a molecular fusion strategy in water solvents. The prepared CDs could be used not only for the assays of Ag+ and L-cysteine (Cys) in aqueous medium with detectable limits of 0.019 μM and 0.015 μM, respectively, but also for cell imaging [20].

As a significant metal ion, silver ion is one of the most harmful ions in polluted water, and it also is harmful to the human body [21,22]. Therefore, it is necessary to establish an accurate, fast and simple detection method for silver ion. Gao et al. adopted simple heat treatment of ethylene glycol solution to get water-soluble carbon dots, and Ag+ would enhance the photoluminescence intensity of the prepared CDs [23]. Zhong et al. obtained CDs by using o-phenylenediamine and amino-formaldehyde and it was the effect of Ag+ that diminished the fluorescence intensity of CDs [24].

Glutathione (GSH) is an important biological thiol with antioxidant effects and integrated detoxification. GSH (especially in hepatocytes) is involved in biotransformation, converting harmful toxic substances in the body into harmless substances that are excreted from the body. Glutathione also helps to maintain the normal function of the immune system [25]. Research has shown that cancer cells have a significantly higher concentration of glutathione than normal tissue cells do. It is based on this feature that provides us with an important basis for the differentiation of cancer cells from normal cells.

In this paper, yellow fluorescent CDs were successfully synthesized by the solvothermal method (240°, 12 h) using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the carbon source, p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) as the nitrogen source, and ethanol as the solvent. The prepared CDs exhibited excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence properties and achieved Ag+ detection in solution. CDs showed excellent anti-interference ability to other metal ions in the detection of Ag+. Glutathione (GSH) selectively restored the fluorescence burst of CDs quenched by Ag+ ions. In addition, the prepared CDs had good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, which could be used as a distinguishing recognition between cancer cells (MCF-7) and normal cells (EC). This method opens up the potential applications of CDs in environmental metal ion detection as well as in biological diagnosis ( Scheme 1).

P-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), O-phenylenediamine (OPD), Glutathione (GSH), NiSO4·6H2O, CaCl2, ZrCl4, CdCl3·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, KCl, MgCl2, MnCl2, NaCl, FeCl3, CuCl2, SnCl2·2H2O, GdCl3·6H2O, AgNO3 were purchased from China Maclean Biochemical Co. We purchased Cell Counting Kit 8 (CCK-8) from Dahlan Meron Biotechnology Co. Cell culture medium, penicillin-streptomycin double antibody solution, phosphate buffered saline (PBS), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and 0.25 % trypsin-EDTA phenol red were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific-CN. Phosphate buffer brine was used as solvent in all experimental solutions. The chemical reagents used in this experiment were all analytical reagents.

Analysis FT-IR spectra were documented on a Nicolet FT-IR 5700 spectrum meter in the field of 1000 ∼ 3600 cm−1. Observation of the morphologies of CDs was done by transmission electron microscopy (TEM JEM-2100). The composition of CDs was described on a Kratos Axis Ultra system using x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrum collection was provided by Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer measurements. CDs fluorescence spectra were all collected using a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. KQ-250DB CNC ultrasonic cleaning machine was taken over for ultrasonic use. The cell imaging profiles were all imaged cytologically using a laser confocal scanning microscope FV100r-ix81, and the cytotoxicity was determined using an enzymatic standard ELx800. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs was determined with an Edinburgh FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrometer.

0.25 g o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and 0.15 g p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) were mixed in 20 mL ethanol solution. Afterwards, the solution was transferred to the sonicator for sonication (5 min), and the sonicated solution was transferred to a 50 mL high-pressure autoclave and put in the oven at 240° for 12 h. After the reaction finished, the solution was cooled down to room temperature and the black-red liquid was filtered through 0.22 mm filter paper for getting rid of the large particles. Thereafter, the reacted solution was permeabilized in dialysis bags (MW:1000) for 48 h. The solution after dialysis was finally filtered through a 0.22 µm filter to remove impurities from the solution to obtain a pure solution. The pure solution was transferred to a freeze dryer and the red-brown powder was obtained by freeze drying.

The optimal excitation wavelength for the detection of Ag+ ions by these CDs was determined by studying the fluorescence change of CDs under different excitation wavelength, and the obtained CDs could produce the optimum emission fluorescence of 560 nm at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. To verify the selectivity of CDs for the detection of Ag+ ions, 13 kinds of metal ions (Ni+, Ca2+, Zr4+, Cd2+, Co2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Gd3+) were selected to study their effects on the fluorescence of CDs. Ag+ or other 13 metal ion solutions was added to the CDs solution (50 μg/mL) separately at room temperature, and the density of metal ions in the mixed solution was quantified as 1 mM. The mixed solutions were incubated at ambient temperature for 2 min. The fluorescence spectrophotometer excitation wavelength was established at 420 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 560 nm. All fluorescence spectra were detected quantitatively in 2 mL of solution standards. All experiments were repeated 3 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of Ag+ ions in different metal ion solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Ag+ ions were added into 13 different metal ions (Ni+, Ca2+, Zr4+, Cd2+, Co2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Gd3+) and CDs mixed solutions respectively, and the density of 13 different metal ions at last mixed liquor was all quantified to 1 mM. The mixed solutions were incubated at room temperature for 2 min. The excitation wavelength of fluorescence spectrophotometer was established at 420 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 560 nm. All experiments were repeated 3 times.

The fluorescence intensity of CDs (50 μg/mL) was investigated when they interacted with different concentrations of silver ion solutions (5–600 mΜ) at ambient temperature. The excitation wavelength of fluorescence spectrophotometer was established at 420 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 560 nm. All experiments were repeated 3 times. The limit of detection was figured out as LOD= 3δ/s (δ means the standard deviation of fluorescence intensity of the samples and s means the standard slope of line).

The fluorescence of CDs was quenched by Ag+ ions adding into the CDs solution. CDs final concentration was set as 50 μg/mL and Ag+ concentration was set as 600 μM. GSH was then added to the mixed solution as a fluorescence switch. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was recorded with the glutathione intensity, and all experiments were repeated 3 times.

Cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed using CCK-8 kit. Cancer cells (MCF-7) and normal cells (EC) were incubation for 24 h in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM). MCF-7 cells and EC cells were seeded in 96-well plates containing 10 % fetal bovine serum and 2 % penicillin-streptomycin (100 μL/well) until the cells were completely adhered to the wall. Each well was then incubated with different concentrations of CDs solution (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150 μg/mL, respectively) for 24 h. CCK-8 (10 μL) was then added into each well and the cells were continued to be incubated for 1 h. The absorbance was measurement at 490 nm with ELx800 microplate reader. Each group had 5 parallel samples.

MCF-7 cells and EC cells were cultured in 20 mm Petri culture dishes containing a DMEM medium for 24 h. The DMEM substrate in the Petri dishes was substituted by 2 mL of DMEM medium which contained the prepared CDs (200 μg/mL). After hatching for 4 h, the medium containing CDs was eliminated and the cells were rinsed three times with PBS buffer. After that, Ag+ (500 μmol/L) was added into the Petri dishes and the cells were incubated for 20 min, followed by adding of GSH (200 μmol/L) and 1 h incubation. The fluorescence imagines of MCF-7 cells incubated with CDs (200 μg/mL), incubated with CDs (200 μg/mL) and Ag+ (500 μM) and incubated with CDs (200 μg/mL), Ag+ (500 μM), and GSH (200 μM) were all recorded by confocal microscopy with 569 nm laser excitation.

CDs were synthesized from OPD and PABA by solvothermal method. Nitrogen doping not only improved the fluorescence-quantum yield of CDs, but also made CDs more hydrophilic. After purification and freeze-drying, a reddish-brown powder was obtained. The aqueous solution of CDs did not precipitate or aggregate for 6 months at room temperature. It showed that CDs had excellent water solubility and structure stability. The morphology and size of CDs were characterized by TEM (shown in Fig. 1(a)). The TEM images of CDs showed spherical nanoparticles, and the lattice stripe interval of CDs was 0.284 nm from HRTEM images. As shown in Fig. 1(b) below, the spherical CDs had better monodispersity. The average particle size of CDs was 2.44 nm, and the particle size distribution ranged from 1.5 to 3.5 nm.

The functional groups associated with the constituent CDs were separately described by FT-IR and XPS. As seen in Fig. 2(a), the FT-IR spectra of CDs showed a feature absorption peak in the stretching vibration of N-H at 3367 cm−1, a feature absorption peak in the stretching vibration of O–H at 3205 cm−1, a feature absorption peak in the asymmetric stretching vibration of CO at 1684 cm−1, a spike in the stretching vibration of CC at 1604 cm−1, and a very distinct spike in the stretching vibration of CN at 1504 cm−1 [26,27]. There were two wide peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 750 cm−1 which responded to the C–H in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibration respectively, and the sharp peak observed at 1275 cm−1 corresponded to the C–O stretching vibration [28]. FT-IR demonstrated that the synthesized carbon dots were abundant in functional groups (–COOH, –NH2, –OH, etc.), which would greatly increase the water solubility of the carbon dots. The CDs chemical composition was investigated by XPS further. As seen in Fig. 2(b), the XPS had three different bond energy peaks homologous to C1s, N1s, and O1s with the content of 74.25 %, 13.54 %, and 12.21 %, respectively. High-resolution energy spectra and peak fitting of the individual elements were seen in Fig. 2(c-e). The three peaks formed by C1s corresponded to C–C/CC (284.9 eV), C–O/C–N (286 eV) and CO/CN (288.7 eV) respectively [29]. As seen in Fig. 2(d), the energy spectrum of O1s had two peaks which were corresponding to C–O (531.6 eV) and CO (523.6 eV), respectively [30]. As seen in Fig. 2(e), the energy spectra of N1s had three fitted peaks at 399 eV, 399.8 eV and 401 eV, corresponding to pyridinic type N, amino type N and pyrrolic type N, respectively. The results of XPS and FT-IR analysis were consistent. The surfaces of CDs contained a high number of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups (such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups), so CDs had good water solubility.

The UV-Vis spectrum of CDs ( Fig. 3(a)) had two absorption peaks at 295 nm and 420 nm, corresponding to the π-π* electronic transition of CC and the n-π* electronic transition of C–N/CN or C–O/CO, respectively [29]. The fluorescence emission spectrum in the figure showed that CDs had a strong emission peak at 560 nm at excitation of 420 nm. The excitation spectrum of CDs overlapped with the long-wavelength UV absorption spectrum (420 nm). This result indicated that the emission of CDs was mainly contributed by the surface nitrogen and oxygen-containing structures [31]. As seen in Fig. 3(b), the emission peak wavelength of CDs (centered at 560 nm) did not vary with the excitation wavelength (from 390 nm to 450 nm). These findings indicated that the fluorescence emission of the prepared CDs was not affected by excitation. which was consistent with the homogeneity of the CDs and surface molecular state [32–34]. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was measured to be 24.6 % using a fluorescence integrating sphere. The high fluorescence quantum yield was not only due to the doping of nitrogen, but also to the fact that the CDs were obtained in a more oh-so-carbon-rich core state throughout the preparation process. During the study we found that different pH values had a large effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the solution of CDs showed different fluorescence intensities when the solution was varied from pH 2.0–12.0. The fluorescence of CDs in a strong acidic environment was severely suppressed when the pH was 2–5, whereas the fluorescence intensity suddenly increased when the pH was 6, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs hardly changed significantly when the pH was in the environment of 6–8. And then the fluorescence intensity of CDs was becoming weaker under alkaline condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity detection of Ag+ ions by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the unique fluorescence properties of CDs, its fluorescence sensing characteristics was explored. The fluorescence response of CDs in the existence of various metal ions was investigated, including Ni+, Ca2+, Zr4+, Cd2+, Co2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Gd3+ and Ag+. As seen in Fig. 4(a), F0 represented the intensity of CDs fluorescence excited at 420 nm and F indicated fluorescence intensity excited at 420 nm in the coexistence of CDs and metal ions. The blank group (carbon quantum dot solution) was served as the control group. The results showed that only Ag+ ions could significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs. The presence of other metal ions had little influence on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4(b), when other metal ion in solution was co-incubated with Ag+ in CDs solution, Ag+ was still able to effectively inhibit the fluorescence emission of CDs, and its inhibitory effect was almost unaffected by other metal ions. This fact indicated the excellent selection of the prepared CDs for detection of Ag+.

The detection ability of CDs for Ag+ was analysed. As seen in Fig. 5(a), the fluorescence intensity of CDs continuously decreased with the increase of silver ion concentration, showing a clear dependence on the concentration of Ag+. When the Ag+ concentration reached 600 μM, an almost complete quenching of fluorescence of CDs was observed. As shown in Fig. 5(b), an excellent linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and silver ion concentration in the range of 0 ∼ 500 μM. The limit concentration of its detection (LOD=3δ/s, where δ represented the standard deviation of the sample and s represented the slope of the standard linearity) was determined as 75 nM.

So as to understand the fluorescence quenching process of CDs, the fluorescence lifetime decay from CDs in the existence/absence of Ag+ was investigated on this basis. The prepared CDs had an average lifetime of 2.04 ns, as illustrated by Fig. 5(c), and the average lifetime of the CDs after co-incubation with Ag+ was 0.64 ns. The result indicated that the lifetime decay with the addition of Ag+ was severe and electron transfer between CDs and Ag+ occurred [35]. This might be attributed to the involvement of lone pairs of electrons from nitrogen and oxygen atoms in CDs in photo-induced electron transfer [36] and the coordination reaction between Ag+ and CN [37], which could thus also indicate that Ag+ had a strong binding ability to functional groups (hydroxyl groups, amino and carboxyl) on the surface of CDs, allowing the presence by Ag+ to attenuate the fluorescence lifetime of CDs.

Previous work had shown that thiol-like amino acids were more likely to form stable bindings with metal ions due to the presence of hydrophobic groups [20,38], Therefore glutathione (GSH) was used to test fluorescence recovery ("on" fluorescence) for the fluorescence quenched CDs/Ag+ system. The pattern of fluorescence on/off/on was shown in the Scheme 1, where the chelation of Ag+ with CDs quenched the CDs’ fluorescence [32,39], and Ag+ was taken away from the surface of CDs after the addition of GSH due to the stronger binding of Ag+ to GSH [40]. In these process, the fluorescence of CDs went from "on" to "off" and then to "on". As seen in Fig. 6(a), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/Ag+ system increased continuously with the increasing concentration of GSH, and there was a significant dependence on the concentration of GSH. As seen in Fig. 6(b), When the GSH concentration was in the range of 1.5 μM to 150 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/Ag+ system showed a well-defined linear relationship with the GSH concentration. The limit concentration of its detection (LOD=3δ/s, where δ represented the standard deviation of the samples and s represented the slope of the standard linearity) was determined as10 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ and GSH in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the intra-cellular imaging potential of CDs, the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of CDs were investigated by incubating CDs with EC cells and MCF-7 cells using the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 7, after EC cells and MCF-7 cells were incubated with different concentration of CDs (0–150 μg/mL) for 24 h, the viability of the cells could be found to remain above 80 % at all times. Thus, the prepared CDs were biocompatible and had low toxicity.

After that, CDs /Ag+ system was used as fluorescent probes to detect intracellular GSH. Cancer cells (MCF-7) were selected for imaging experiments, and were then viewed under a laser confocal microscope excited at 569 nm. As seen in Fig. 8(a), CDs had clear images in the cells. As shown in Fig. 8(b), little to no fluorescent signal in the cell was seen when the incubated cells were treated with Ag+. As seen in Fig. 8(c), the yellow fluorescence in the cells was effectively restored when the cells were treated with GSH. This result suggested that the prepared CDs/Ag+ system could be used as a potential fluorescent probe for the detection of GSH in living cells.

It has been shown that the level of GSH in cancer cells is significantly higher than that in normal cells [41,42]. Therefore, the CDs/Ag+ system with excellent GSH fluorescence enhancement properties was further employed to differentiate cancer cells from normal cells. As seen in Fig. 9, MCF-7 cancer cells treated with CDs/Ag+/GSH had stronger fluorescence emission than normal EC cell cells treated with CDs/Ag+/GSH under the same conditions. This suggested that the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe system could be significantly increased in containing high concentrations of glutathione in cancer cells which was formally reported previously [43,44]. The significant differences in the fluorescence imaging of these two types of cells facilitated the ability to identify normal and cancer cells.

In summary, nitrogen-doped yellow fluorescent CDs had been easily prepared by PABA, OPD, and ethanol using a solvothermal method and were used as a fluorescent sensor for Ag+ ion/GSH monitor and differentiation of cancer cells (MCF-7) from normal cells (EC). The prepared CDs had good photoluminescence properties and exhibited significant excitation-independent properties. The CDs underwent significant quenching of their fluorescence when mixed with metal Ag+, making CDs have a highly sensitive Ag+ ion detection limit of 75 nM. Meanwhile, CDs also showed good anti-interference ability, and the quenching ability of Ag+ ions on CDs fluorescence did not change greatly when other different metal ion solutions were added to the CDs/Ag+ system. In addition, the fluorescence intensity was significantly restored when GSH was added to the CDs/Ag+ system with the detection limit of 10 nM. Since the concentration of GSH in cancer cells was much more than that in normal cells, CDs could effectively discriminate between cancer cells and normal cells. Thus, the prepared CDs are expected to be used for Ag+ ion detection and glutathione detection as well as biological diagnosis.
This paper follows all research ethics standards and procedures.

Y Y Z directed the whole experiment and led the manuscript revision. X K L completed most of the experiments, data analysis, and manuscript writing. X H, Y X, X J L and H Z participated in some experiments. J C, J P and C L provided partial experimental comments. All authors have read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51802282) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ17F040004).

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-P. Sun., B. Zhou., Y. Lin., W. Wang., K.A.S. Fernando., P. Pathak., P. Pathak., B.A. Harruff., X. Wang., H. Wang., P.G. Luo., H. Yang., M.E. Kose., B. Chen., L.M. Veca., S.-Y. Xie., uantum-Sized Carbon Dots for Bright and Colorful Photoluminescence, JOURNAL OF THE AMERICAN CHEMICAL SOCIETY 128 (2006).

Nanoscale

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots for naked eye colorimetric ultrasensitive arsenic and glutathione detection
Biosens. Bioelectron.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sequential ‘ON-OFF-ON’ fluorescence probe for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ and l-alanine/l-histidine
J. Photochem. Photobiol. A: Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots derived from red beetroot as a selective probe for Pd2+ detection
J. Photochem. Photobiol. A: Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications
